2: hayyām mûṣāq — ‘the cast sea’; the large bronze basin. mûṣāq is a participle of yāṣaq (to pour, cast metal), describing the manufacturing method: poured/cast bronze rather than hammered. The yām (sea) is the common word for the Mediterranean Sea — the choice of ‘sea’ for the basin reflects either its immense size (nearly 5 meters in diameter) or a cosmological symbolism (the primordial waters controlled). The dimensions given in 2 Chronicles 4:2 (10 cubits in diameter, 5 cubits in height, 30 cubits in circumference) produce a mathematical discrepancy with π: a 10-cubit diameter circle has a circumference of 31.4 cubits, not 30. Ancient mathematical approximations (using π ≈ 3) account for the difference; ancient near Eastern architectural descriptions used round numbers for circumference. The MKT renders ‘the cast bronze sea,’ including both the material and the manufacturing note to help the reader understand the scale and weight of the object.
6: lᵉrāḥṣāh / lᵉrāḥṣāh ʾotām — ‘for washing’ / ‘for washing them [the sacrifices].’ The distinction between the lavers (for washing the parts of the burnt offering) and the sea (for the priests’ own washing) marks a functional differentiation within the water-purification system. The lavers (kiyyōrôt) are for the offerings; the sea (yām) is for the priests. The differentiation reflects the graded-holiness structure of the sanctuary: sacrificial materials require one form of purification; the priests require another. The Levitical purity regulations (Num 19; Lev 11-15) govern both categories. The MKT renders ‘for rinsing what is used in the burnt offering’ and ‘for the priests to wash in,’ making the distinction functionally explicit.
1: wayyaʿaś mizbah neḥōšet ʿeśrîm ammāh ʾorkô wᵉʿeśrîm ammāh roḥbô wᵉʿeśer ammôt qomātô — ‘He made a bronze altar, twenty cubits long, twenty cubits wide, and ten cubits high.’ The bronze altar of the temple is approximately 15 times the area of the Mosaic tabernacle altar (5×5×3 cubits, Exodus 27:1). The scale increase is proportional to Israel’s expansion from mobile desert community to settled national kingdom: the altar must accommodate the vastly increased volume of national sacrifice. The 20×20 cubit base (identical in width and length to the Most Holy Place, 2 Chronicles 3:8) gives the altar the same footprint as the sanctuary’s innermost room — a theological proportion: the sacrifice-locus and the divine-presence locus are equally scaled, because the sacrifice is what makes access to the divine presence possible.
3: ûḏᵉmût ʾᵉgālîm sabbîb taḥtāyw — ‘Around the outside were figures of oxen encircling it, ten per cubit, all around.’ The ox-imagery around the sea base (the twelve oxen supporting the sea, 2 Chronicles 4:4, and the encircling ox-figures) connects the basin to the imagery of strength, agricultural provision, and sacral power. In Canaanite iconography the bull was associated with El (the chief deity); Israel’s use of ox-imagery for the sacred sea represents the reappropriation of Canaanite visual vocabulary in service of YHWH-worship — a phenomenon also visible in the golden calf incident, which tried to represent YHWH through the bull-image. The ten-per-cubit specification gives the encircling ox-frieze a density of forty oxen per row in two rows (2 Chronicles 4:3b: ‘the oxen were in two rows, cast as one piece with the Sea’).
4: ʿōmēd ʿal-šᵉnêm ʿāśār bāqār šᵉlōšāh pônîm ṣāpônāh ûšlōšāh pônîm yāmmāh — ‘It rested on twelve oxen — three facing north, three facing west, three facing south, and three facing east.’ The twelve oxen in four groups of three represent the twelve tribes of Israel arranged around the central sacred object — a spatial enacted of the twelve-tribe covenant structure. The same fourfold arrangement of threes echoes the wilderness camp: three tribes on each side of the tabernacle (Num 2). The sea rests on the twelve-tribe base, indicating that the entire covenant people (kol-yiśrāʾēl) supports the priestly water-rite. The NT church inherited this twelve-tribe structure in the twelve apostles, who serve as the foundation-stones of the new covenant community (Revelation 21:14).
5: ûqômātô wᵉśiptô kᵉmaʿăśēh śepat kôs pereḥ šûšannāh — ‘Its walls were a handbreadth thick, and its rim was shaped like the rim of a cup, like a lily flower (pereḥ šûšannāh) in bloom.’ The lily-shaped rim (šûšannāh = lily, lotus) gives the functional bronze basin an organic elegance. The lily appears on the pillar-capitals (1 Kings 7:19,22), on the sea’s rim, and as an architectural motif throughout the temple. The lily-lotus is a symbol of purity, renewal, and life in ancient Near Eastern iconography — applied to water vessels in the sanctuary, it suggests the purifying, life-giving quality of the ritual water. mahazîq bat ʾalpayim (holding three thousand baths) — the Chronicler’s 3,000 baths vs. 1 Kings 7:26’s 2,000 baths is either a textual variant or a distinction between filled-to-capacity and normal operating volume.
7: wayyaʿaś ʾet-mᵉnôrôt hazzāhāḇ ʿeśer kammišpaṭ ûmᵉnôrôt — ‘He made ten gold lampstands according to the prescribed pattern (kammišpaṭ).’ The single Mosaic tabernacle lampstand (Exodus 25:31-40: the seven-branched mᵉnōrāh) is multiplied tenfold for the temple: ten lampstands, five per side. kammišpaṭ (according to the specification, the rule) — the lampstands conform to the prescribed Mosaic design, scaled up. The mᵉnōrāh’s seven branches represent the seven-fold light of creation (Genesis 1:14-16: luminaries to give light) functioning in the sanctuary — the lampstands’ ten-fold multiplication reflects the temple’s expanded scale while maintaining the Mosaic pattern. The NT’s seven lampstands in Revelation (2 Chronicles 1:12,20) represent the seven churches as light-bearers in the world — the temple’s lampstand-ministry carried into the church age.
8: wayyaʿaś šulḥānôt ʿăśārāh wayyannēaḥ bahêkāl ḥāmiššāh mîmîn waḥāmēššāh miśśemôl — ‘He made ten tables and set them in the temple, five on the south side and five on the north.’ The Mosaic tabernacle had one table for the bread of the Presence (Exodus 25:23-30); the temple multiplies it tenfold. The bread of the Presence (leḥem happānîm) is placed perpetually before YHWH as a covenant-meal symbol: Israel’s ongoing communion with YHWH expressed through the perpetual table. wayyaʿaś mizrᵉqê zāhāb meʾāh (he also made a hundred gold basins) — the hundred gold sprinkling-bowls (mizrᵉqîm) are used for receiving and presenting the blood of sacrifices at the altar; their gold material and large number indicate the high volume of blood-rite worship the temple was designed to accommodate.
9: wayyaʿaś ḥăṣar hakkōhᵃnîm wᵉhāʿăzārāh haggᵉḏôlāh — ‘He built the court of the priests and the large outer court, with bronze-covered doors on the outer court.’ The graded-court system — Most Holy Place → Holy Place → Priests’ Court → Great/Outer Court — encodes the holiness-gradient in spatial terms: the further from the divine presence-zone, the more persons and activities are permitted. The ḥăṣar hakkōhᵃnîm (priests’ court) corresponds to the inner court of 1 Kings 6:36; the ʿăzārāh haggᵉḏôlāh (great court) is the outer precinct accessible to the lay Israelites. The later Herodian temple formalized the court-gradation with named areas (Court of Women, Court of Israel, Court of Priests, Holy of Holies), ultimately adding a Court of Gentiles.
10: wᵉʾet-hayyām nātan mikkātēp hayᵉmānît qēḏmāh mimmûl negeb — ‘He positioned the great sea on the south side of the house, at the southeast corner.’ The sea’s positioning — southeast of the temple — places it in the zone between the temple building and the great altar. The priests would pass it on their way from the altar to the temple entrance, washing before entering the sacred space (consistent with the Mosaic requirement in Exodus 30:17-21 that Aaron and his sons wash their hands and feet before approaching the altar or entering the tent). The topographic placement is therefore liturgically functional: the sea is positioned for the ritual movement of the priests in their daily service.
11: wayyaʿaś ḥûrām et-hassîrôt wᵉʾet-hayy ᵃʿîm wᵉʾet-hammizrāqôt waykkal ḥûrām laʿăśôt — ‘Huram made the pots, shovels, and basins. So Huram completed all the work he undertook for King Solomon on the house of God.’ The summary of Huram’s completed work (waykkal ḥûrām laʿăśôt, Huram finished doing) echoes the completion-formula of the tabernacle: ‘Moses finished the work’ (Exodus 40:33; waykkal Mōšeh ʾet-hammᵉlāʾkāh). The deliberate parallel frames Huram-abi’s craftsman-completion as the temple equivalent of Bezalel’s tabernacle-completion — confirming the temple as the tabernacle’s successor and Huram-abi as Bezalel’s functional successor.
12: ʾet-šᵉnê hāʿammûḏîm wᵉhagullôt wᵉhakkapitōrôt — ‘the two pillars, the bowl-shaped capitals on top of them, and the two networks of chains that covered the bowl-shaped capitals.’ The inventory of Huram’s work provides a technical catalog of the bronze components. gullôt (bowl-capitals) — from gāl (wheel, bowl, round object) — are the rounded upper sections of the pillar capitals. kapittōrîm (capitals, crown-pieces) — the same word used for the knob-caps on the tabernacle lampstand (Exodus 25:31-36: kapittōr translated ‘knob’). The shared vocabulary between the lampstand ornaments and the pillar-capitals links the portable tabernacle furniture and the permanent temple architectural elements within a shared visual vocabulary.
13: wᵉʾarbaʿ meʾôt rimmônîm lišŠᵉtê hassᵉḇākôt — ‘four hundred pomegranates for the two networks — two rows per network.’ The pomegranate-count totals 400 (100 per row × 2 rows × 2 pillars): the same figure given in 1 Kings 7:20. The Chronicler’s detailed accounting of the pomegranate total serves the inventory’s role as a record of completion: this number of ornaments was produced and installed. The pomegranate’s association with the priestly vestments (Exodus 28:33-34) and its placement on the temple pillars creates a visual continuity between the priest who enters the sanctuary and the sanctuary’s entrance: both are marked by the same fertility-and-fullness symbol.
14: wᵉʾet-hammᵉkônôt wᵉʾet-hakiyyōrôt ʿal-hammᵉkônôt — ‘He made the movable stands and the basins mounted on them.’ The mᵉkônôt (wheeled stands) are the elaborate ten-stand construction described in 1 Kings 7:27-39 — each with panels decorated with cherubim, lions, and palms, mounted on four bronze wheels. The wheeled design (ʾôpannîm, wheels) allows the lavers to be moved to wherever the priestly washing needs are, giving the system a mobility that the tabernacle tradition valued. The inventory at this point (2 Chronicles 4:14-15) is a brief summary of the detailed 1 Kings account, sufficient for the Chronicler’s purposes without reproducing the full technical specification.
15: ʾet-hayyām hāʾeḥāḏ wᵉʾet-habbāqār šᵉnêm ʿāśār taḥtāyw — ‘one great sea with the twelve oxen beneath it.’ The sea reappears in the summary list to anchor the complete water-system: the single sea (for priestly washing) + the twelve oxen supporting it + the ten lavers (for sacrificial washing) constitute the temple’s complete purification infrastructure. The twelve-oxen base of the sea is mentioned separately from the ten lavers to emphasize the sea’s unique status as the priestly purification basin — it rests on the covenant-twelve, not merely on an architectural support.
16: hassîrôt wᵉhayyāʿîm wᵉhammizlāgôt wᵉkol-kᵉlêhem ʿāśāh ḥûrām ʾābîw lᵉhammelek šᵉlōmōh — ‘The pots, shovels, meat forks, and all related equipment that Huram-abi made for King Solomon for the LORD’s house — all were of polished bronze.’ neḥōšet mārûq (polished/burnished bronze) — the careful finishing of the utensils reflects the same concern for quality evident in the gold-overlay of the interior. Even the cooking-and-cleaning implements are made from the best available metal, finished to a high standard. The Mosaic tabernacle used bronze extensively for the altar and its implements (Exodus 27:1-8; 2 Chronicles 38:1-7); the temple perpetuates this sacred-metal convention at a scale commensurate with the permanent sanctuary.
17: bᵉkikkar haYarden yᵉṣāqām hammelek bᵉʾăḏamat hammār bên Sukkôt ûḇên Ṣᵉrêḏāh — ‘The king had them cast in the clay ground of the Jordan plain, between Succoth and Zeredah.’ The Jordan valley clay-ground (between Succoth and Zeredah, east of the Jordan in what is now modern Jordan) provided optimal conditions for large-scale bronze-casting: good clay for molds, proximity to fuel (the forests of Transjordan), and the Jordan River for cooling and water-supply. The choice of a remote casting-site away from Jerusalem also protected the city from the industrial-scale fire hazards of large metal-foundry operations. The location-specific detail is characteristic of the Chronicler’s interest in authenticity and historical precision.
18: wayyaʿaś šᵉlōmōh ʾet-kol-hakkēlîm hāʾēlleh lᵉrōb meʾōḏ kî lo-nḥqar mišqal haneḥōšet — ‘Solomon made all these vessels in such quantity that the total weight of the bronze was never calculated.’ lōʾ neḥqar mišqal haneḥōšet (the weight of the bronze was not investigated/calculated) — the bronze is beyond accounting. This deliberate non-accounting parallels the non-accounting of silver and gold in 2 Chronicles 1:15 (silver as common as stones). The temple’s material extravagance is expressed through quantitative excess: weight and number become incalculable. The rhetorical function is doxological: to say ‘we stopped counting’ is to say ‘there was too much to count’ — abundance beyond enumeration as a form of praise.
19: wayyaʿaś šᵉlōmōh ʾēt kol-hakkēlîm ʾăšer bêt hāʾelōhîm wᵉʾēt mizbaḥ hazzāhāḇ wᵉʾēt haššulḥānôt wᵉʿălêhem leḥem happānîm — ‘Solomon also made all the furnishings for the house of God: the golden altar, the tables for the bread of the Presence.’ The golden altar (mizbaḥ hazzāhāḇ) is the incense altar — distinct from the bronze burnt-offering altar of 2 Chronicles 4:1. The pairing of incense altar and bread-of-the-Presence table in the first mention of the golden furnishings reflects the interior court’s two permanent liturgical installations: the incense continually ascending as a fragrant offering (Exodus 30:7-8) and the bread continually set before YHWH (Exodus 25:30). Both represent ongoing covenant relationship: the ascending fragrance (prayer) and the shared table (fellowship) — the two constitutive elements of the covenant relationship.
20: wᵉʾet-hammᵉnōrôt wᵉnērōtêhem liḇʿōr kammišpāṭ lipnê haddᵉḇîr zāhāb sāgûr — ‘the lampstands of pure gold (zāhāb sāgûr) with their lamps to burn before the inner sanctuary, as the specifications required.’ zāhāb sāgûr (closed/pure gold, refined gold) — the superlative of gold quality: gold that has been completely refined, without alloy. The lamps burning before the inner sanctuary (dᵉḇîr) represent the perpetual-light provision for the Most Holy Place, which had no windows and was in complete darkness except for the lampstand-light. The kammišpāṭ (according to the specification) again invokes Mosaic authorization: the lampstands replicate the Mosaic design at the temple scale.
21: wᵉhappᵉrāḥîm wᵉhannērôt wᵉhammᵉlaqqāḥayim zāhāb hûʾ miklāl zāhāb — ‘the floral decorations, lamps, and tongs of purest gold (miklāl zāhāb, perfect gold).’ miklāl is a hapax that apparently intensifies gold’s quality — possibly ‘complete,’ ‘perfect,’ or ‘all-gold’ (as opposed to gold-plated). The floral decorations (pᵉrāḥîm, flowers/blossoms) on the lampstand evoke the almond-blossom motif of the Mosaic lampstand (Exodus 25:33-34: ‘like almond blossoms’) — the lampstand is designed to look like a flowering tree, combining the tree-of-life and light-of-creation motifs in one object.
22: ûhammᵉzammᵉrôt wᵉhammizrāqôt wᵉhakappôt wᵉhammahᵃtôt zāhāb sāgûr ûpetaḥ habbayit daltōtāyw hahᵉpᵉnîmiyyôt lᵉqōḏeš haqqŏḏāšîm — ‘the wick trimmers, sprinkling bowls, ladles, and fire pans of pure gold; and the door sockets of the temple — the inner doors to the Most Holy Place.’ The catalog concludes with the most intimate sacred objects: the implements for tending the lampstand flames, handling the sacred blood, and managing the incense fire — all of pure gold. The door sockets (pitaḥ habbayit daltōtāyw) represent the physical threshold mechanism — the hinges and sockets on which the Most Holy Place’s doors swung. Even the mechanical-functional elements (door hardware) are made of pure gold, because every material component of the Most Holy Place participates in the holiness of the space. The inventory ends at the threshold of the Most Holy Place — the same boundary that will be opened by the glory-cloud in 2 Chronicles 5:14.
